narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 46% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to raise concerns about your long-term success. You have some strong points in your profile, like an impressive undergraduate GPA of 3.7—well above the typical 3.21 average—and you're a white female from a lower-middle income family, which aligns closely with many applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile rank of 3 falls below the average of 5.71, your LSAT score of 34 is under the usual 36.7, and your Year 3 decile of 1 is much lower than the typical 5.53, while planning to study part-time puts you in a smaller group compared to the 93% who go full-time. When the model weighed these, your Year 1 decile rank emerged as the single biggest influence, nudging the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind it was your LSAT score, which similarly pulled the odds in that direction. Your Year 3 decile rank came next in importance, reinforcing the concern about inconsistent academic performance in law school. Family income level played a notable role too, followed by your undergraduate GPA, racial background, study status, and gender, each contributing in descending order to the overall risk assessment—all tilting toward a higher failure likelihood compared to students who typically pass. While your strong undergrad record is a positive standout against the average, it wasn't enough to offset these other factors in the model's eyes. Looking ahead, you could realistically retake the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it's a key lever many improve with focused prep. Committing to full-time study might also help, given how rare part-time paths are among successful bar passers. Most importantly, addressing patterns in your law school grades—like seeking tutoring or study groups early—could rebuild that decile momentum for future evaluations or bar prep. With targeted effort on these changeable areas, you'd stand a much better chance next time. Hang in there; many students turn predictions like this around with persistence.
